perturbation.original_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall be indicated as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION may be indicated as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' makes the indication of confidentiality optional, undermining the enforceability of the agreement. The original text established a mandatory process of marking confidential information, this change makes it discretionary, which goes against best practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the requirement for identifying confidential information, potentially hindering the ability to claim trade secret protection under federal law. Under 18 U.S. Code § 1839, proper marking and safeguarding are important factors in determining whether information qualifies as a trade secret. The change makes it so that the company is not required to mark confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' directly contradicts the original intent of ensuring confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that this modification 'weakens the requirement for identifying confidential information, potentially hindering the ability to claim trade secret protection under federal law.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides no direct information, the Law Explanation provides enough justification to show that by making the marking of confidential information optional, the contract undermines its ability to protect trade secrets under federal law, a deviation from legally recognized definitions, which is the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to accept the CONFIDENTI AL INFORMATION and to employ all reasonable efforts to maintain the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION as secret and Page 2 of 3 confidential, such efforts to be no less than the degree of care employed by RECIPIENT to preserve and safeguard RECIPIENT's own confidential informa tion.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees to accept the CONFIDENTI AL INFORMATION and to employ efforts to maintain the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION as confidential, but such efforts do not need to be the degree of care employed by RECIPIENT to preserve and safeguard RECIPIENT's own confidential informa tion.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the standard of care required to protect confidential information from 'reasonable efforts, no less than the degree of care employed by recipient' to something less than that undermines the purpose of the NDA. It creates ambiguity and reduces the protection afforded to the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy for information to be considered a trade secret. Lowering the standard of care makes it more difficult to prove that reasonable efforts were made, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status. UTSA § 1(4) discusses the definition of Misappropriation, which this modification could impede a party from defending against.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a 'degree of care' no less than that which the recipient uses for their own confidential information. The changed text eliminates this standard, potentially allowing a lower level of protection. The Law Explanation indicates that the UTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy for trade secrets. By weakening the standard of care, the changed text introduces a contradiction, as it makes it harder to prove that 'reasonable efforts' were made, which could jeopardize trade secret status under the UTSA. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly address the change, the Law Explanation coupled with the Perturbation Explanation support the conclusion that the changed text creates a contradiction by undermining the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: It is hereby acknowledged by PROVIDER that RECIPIENT shall incur no liability merely for examining and considering the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. However, RECIPIENT agrees that it will not use the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for any purpose other than the PURPOSE without the prior written consent of PROVIDER.
perturbation.changed_text: It is hereby acknowledged by PROVIDER that RECIPIENT shall incur no liability for examining and considering the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. However, RECIPIENT agrees that it will not use the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for any purpose other than the PURPOSE without the prior written consent of PROVIDER.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'merely' significantly broadens the liability waiver. Now, instead of only being protected from liability for examining the information, the recipient is free from all liability regarding examining and considering the confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could be interpreted as limiting the recipient's liability even for actions that could be considered trade secret misappropriation under the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S.C. § 1832), if those actions fall under 'examining and considering' the information. The change limits prosecution of any potential trade secret theft, and is only limited by the purpose stated in the NDA.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the RECIPIENT incurs no liability 'merely' for examining and considering confidential information. The changed text removes 'merely,' significantly broadening the liability waiver. The Law Explanation suggests this change could limit prosecution of trade secret theft under the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S.C. § 1832). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1832, addresses 'Theft of trade secrets' and indicates that whoever with intent to convert a trade secret to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner, is in violation of this law. By removing 'merely,' the changed text provides a broader liability shield that could be interpreted to protect the RECIPIENT even if their actions constitute trade secret theft, contradicting the legal protections outlined in the Economic Espionage Act.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT‘s obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 above shall not extend to any part of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: (e) that is required by any law, regulation, or order of court to be disclosed by RECIPIENT . Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall first not ify and provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT‘s obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 above shall not extend to any part of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: (e) that is required by any law, regulation, or order of court to be disclosed by RECIPIENT . Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall not be required to notify the PROVIDER or provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the RECIPIENT to notify the PROVIDER before disclosing confidential information due to a legal obligation conflicts with the common law principle of mitigating damages and acting in good faith. While the recipient is not bound by the NDA in this specific instance, failing to provide notice eliminates the PROVIDER's opportunity to seek a protective order or other remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change removes a safeguard that allows the disclosing party to potentially seek a protective order under FRCP 26(c) or similar state rules. By removing the notification requirement, the agreement hinders the PROVIDER's ability to protect its confidential information from becoming public during legal proceedings. FRCP 26(c) allows parties to seek protection from discovery, but the PROVIDER must be aware of the impending disclosure to act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the recipient to notify the provider before disclosing confidential information, which directly hinders the provider's ability to seek a protective order under FRCP 26(c) or similar state rules. The Law Explanation states the PROVIDER must be aware of the impending disclosure to act. While Snippet 1 focuses on required disclosures and doesn't explicitly mandate notification in all situations, the removal of the notification clause prevents the PROVIDER from utilizing legal mechanisms to protect their information during legal proceedings. Thus, the modification contradicts legal principles related to protecting confidential information during discovery and acting in good faith to mitigate damages.
